draft.introduction: Attributed to Moses, this is the oldest psalm in the collection. It meditates on divine eternity versus human transience—“a thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday.”

translation.text: Lord, you have been our dwelling place in all generations. Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever you had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting you are God. You return man to dust and say, Return, O children of man!
translation.original_text: κύριε καταφυγὴ ἐγενήθης ἡμῖν ἐν γενεᾷ καὶ γενεᾷ

translation.text: The years of our life are seventy, or even by reason of strength eighty; yet their span is but toil and trouble. So teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom.
translation.original_text: αἱ ἡμέραι τῶν ἐτῶν ἡμῶν ἐν αὐτοῖς ἑβδομήκοντα ἔτη

translator_notes.note: “From everlasting to everlasting” (ἀπὸ τοῦ αἰῶνος καὶ ἕως τοῦ αἰῶνος) uses αἰών twice, a term the Gnostics later appropriated for their emanated divine beings.
